courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Comparative-Historical Method in Educational Research
courses.description: This module is designed to provide a systematic explication to the theoretical perspectives and research methods of comparative and historical study in the social sciences and education. It aims to familiarize students with the methodology of comparative and historical studies on societal and/ or institutional level. The modules will cover topics such as: conception of Verstehen and the theoretical foundation of interpretative research, the logic and assumptions of comparative and historical research, methods and procedures in comparative and historical research, and review of local and foreign examples of comparative and historical studies in education and the social sciences.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Eng Lang Teach, MSc Math Edu, MA Sch Guid & Counsel, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to the course: Historical method in education research; the relationship between History and Sociology; Historical consciousness-conceptualizing or theorising time, structure and agency
2. Constructionist perspective on historical method: archives, oral history, historical records and selection bias
3. Comparative analysis of marco-social phenomena: Why C-H analysis? case studies, logic of comparison: variation across space and time, non-experimental research design, units of analysis, conceptual equivalence, comparable measures, Galton’s problem
4. Comparative-historical methodology: historical sociology and comparative methods; the way of huge comparisons of big structures and large processes; dialogue of ideas and evidence, (Ideal type, necessary and sufficient causes, multiple conjunctural causation, and counterfactual reasoning, path dependence, contingency, temporality, events; causal explanation/ interpretation; deterministic vs probalistic causation, inference/ reasoning based on a small number of cases; trajectory; typology)
5. Comparative-historical methodology: Cont’d
6. Back to philosophy of history again: The nature and status of historical knowledge: fact/ truth, time; Post-modern Historiography; Deconstructionist perspective on historical method: hermeneutics, narrative, discourse, metahistory and theory
7. Conclusion

courses.title: Ethnographic Study in Educational Research
courses.description: This course is designed for students engaged in independent research. It provides students with a systematic explication of the theoretical backgrounds and research methods of ethnographic study in education and social sciences. It aims to familiarize students with the conceptual tools, research procedures and methods of ethnographic studies on organization, group or individual level. The topic to be covered in the module are : conception of Verstehen and the theoretical foundation of interpretative research, phenomenologists an interactionists contributions to ethnography study, methods and procedures of ethnographic study, review of local and foreign examples of ethnographic studies in education and social sciences.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Administrative and Organizational Theory
courses.description: Successful leaders, managers, and administrators underpin their behaviours and practices with a strong foundation of theoretical knowledge and conceptual ability. Accordingly, this course aims at developing students’ theoretical and conceptual abilities. It introduces students to a range of important administrative and organizational theories and perspectives which have influenced management practice at all levels, from system to school, over the past century. These theories and perspectives adopt a wide purview, including structural, human resource, political, symbolic and cultural dimensions. Opportunities will be provided for students to apply the theories and perspectives to current education policy and school management issues in Hong Kong.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Curriculum: Perspectives and Design
courses.description: This course focuses on the inquiry of: 1) different perspectives on curriculum, such as: transmission/ transaction position, and objective/ process model; 2) the application of the above perspectives on the decisions of the nature and relationship of curriculum common places, such as: the selection of aims, goals and objectives, the selection of learning opportunities, the organization of learning opportunities, student evaluation; and 3) decisions on the change of the nature of curriculum common places after the introduction of compulsory schooling and recurrent education.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Eng Lang Teach, MSc Math Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, MA Prof Edu Psy, Doctor of Edu, PhD Edu & Master of Education Teach Key Sub Area (NETTI)
courses.course_syllabus:
\*Weeks 1-7 are designed in a way to help us better understand our common practices of curriculum design and implementation. Weeks 9 - 13 are designed in a way to explore how we can do it better with latest curriculum development theories and practices. That is, how can we teachers learn to become researchers so as to draw out the theoretical significance of our daily curriculum practices. Please also note that the topics and readings may change along the semester, if needed.

Week 1
Topic: Introduction to ‘curriculum’ and this course 課程的概念、意義及領域
Discussion Questions:What counts as ‘curriculum’, ‘curriculum consciousness’ + ‘critical thinking’? 課程領域有哪些範疇？課程意識和批判性思維如何培養？
Case: Pedagogical examples in HK and Mainland China classrooms

Week 2
Topic: Teaching-learning motivation and engagement 課程、教學與學習的關係
Discussion Questions: What are TARGET and ABC models? 課程、教學與學習之間有何關係？
Case: Classroom engagement analysis

Week 3
Topic: Backward Design Model + Tyler rationale I: Selecting learning goals 後向設計模型 + 泰勒原理四要素之一：學習目標
Discussion Questions: Why do we learn Tyler Rationale? How do we select learning goals? 為什麼需要學習泰勒原理？選取課程目標時需要考慮甚麼？
Case: Learning goals in HK & Mainland China/ University outcome-based approach

Week 4
Topic: Tyler Rationale II: Selecting learning experiences 泰勒原理四要素之二：學習體驗／經驗（課程內容）的選取
Discussion Questions: How do we select learning experiences? 有甚麼理論協助我們選取合宜的教學內容？
Case: 21st Core Competencies/ Skills

Week 5
Topic: Tyler Rationale III: Organizing learning experiences 泰勒原理四要素之三：課程組織（垂直水平、統整與分科）
Discussion Questions: How do we organize learning experiences? Horizontal & Vertical, Integrated & Subject 學習體驗／經驗如何組織？水平或垂直？統整與分科？
Case: Chinese, Math, and STEAM curricula

Week 6
Topic: Tyler Rationale IV: Evaluation and assessment 泰勒原理四要素之四：課程和教學評估 Possible guest speaker: TBD
Discussion Questions: How do we assess student learning? Formative vs. Summative Assessment 如何評估學生學習？形成性評估／總結性評估

Week 7
Topic: Guest lecture by Mrs. Dee Mulligan on Backward Design Model and Best Practice
Discussion Questions: Using Backward design model for unit design 思考後向設計與泰勒原理的關係

Week 8
Topic: Mid-term Project Presentation Conference
Discussion Questions: Share your unit design experiences with peers

Week 9
Topic: Alternative curriculum perspective and design 1: Joseph Schwab
Discussion Questions: Limits of Tyler Rationale + From Outcome to Context 泰勒原理的侷限性？從目標轉向場域
Case: Bohunt school incident

Week 10
Topic: Alternative curriculum perspective and design 2: William Pinar + Dwayne Huebner
Discussion Questions: Humanistic/ Cultural Regard in Curriculum 課程的人文文化性
Case: Curriculum subjects + language

Week 11
Topic: Alternative curriculum perspective and design 3: 佐藤學 & 歐用生
Discussion Questions: From Knowledge to Value; Mind to Body + Study從知識轉向價值；從理智轉向身體
Case: Embodied curriculum

Week 12
Topic: Latest curriculum development in HK and Mainland China + Curriculum theory and practice + Course review 課程理論和實踐
Discussion Questions: Cases: STEAM, e-learning, differentiated mode of instruction
Rethinking curriculum consciousness & teachers as researchers 再思課程意識的培養

Week 13
Topic: Guest Lecture by Dr. Marty Schmidt
Discussion Questions: Designing curricula for social conscience and inner awakening: Personal reflections

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Curriculum Evaluation: Theory and Practice
courses.description: Curriculum evaluation has an important role in the curriculum development process. This course aims at helping the participants broaden and deepen their understanding of the major theoretical, political and ethical issues in curriculum evaluation. It also focuses on the principles and skills of planning, designing and managing curriculum evaluation studies. Examples and case studies will be chosen in accordance with the background of the participants.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Eng Lang Teach, MSc Math Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, MA Prof Edu Psy, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.course_syllabus:
Topics include: purposes of curriculum evaluation; criteria for meta-evaluation; evaluating the intended curriculum; evaluating the implemented curriculum; evaluating the learned curriculum; evaluation models; and evaluation designs.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Teaching: Orientation, Strategy and Application
courses.description: This course is designed to deepen and broaden participants' understanding of teaching, learning and knowledge. Characteristics that distinguish teaching approaches, divergent perceptions of learning, theories of knowledge acquisition, and theory of variation will be explored. Knowledge base of teachers, conceptualization of teachers' knowledge, teaching strategies, and constructive pedagogy will be discussed. Participants will be guided to reflect on their teaching practice and examine how teachers' knowledge can be studied, how teaching can be evaluated, and how class learning can be assessed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Eng Lang Teach, MSc Math Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, MA Prof Edu Psy, Doctor of Edu, PhD Edu & Master of Education Teach Key Sub Area (NETTI)
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:
1. identify distinctive features characterizing teaching approaches, learning perceptions, and forms of knowledge
2. conceptualize teachers’ knowledge base and structure
3. comprehend the various ways of assessing teaching
4. reflect on their teaching practice

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)
courses.course_syllabus:
- Defining teaching; reasons underpinning one’s choice to teach
- Defining constructivism; cognitive constructivism vs. social constructivism; Zone of Proximal Development, scaffolding
- Curriculum orientation and the impacts on teachers’ teaching strategies
- Theories of action: espoused theory and theories-in-action; double-loop learning and single-loop learning; transmission, transaction, transformation; surface approach, and achieving approach to learning
- Identify the distinctive features characterizing teaching approaches ranging from teacher-directed to learner-centered
- Theories of knowledge: epistemology, rationalism, empiricism; Shulman’s seven knowledge domains; possible ways to study teachers’ knowledge
- Teaching quality, teaching competence, teaching effectiveness, and teacher efficacy; ways to assessing teaching quality

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Curriculum Change and Implementation
courses.description: This course focuses on equipping curriculum developers and senior teachers responsible for curriculum matters in schools with the theories and practical skills of managing curriculum change. Since designing a curriculum without successful implementation is a waste of time and resources, the present curriculum development and implementation process in Hong Kong will be critically analysed. Curriculum implement theories and experiences from other countries will be introduced to shed light on possible adaptation for handling curriculum change more effectively both at the territory-wide and school level.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: The English Language Curriculum
courses.description: This course is designed to raise students’ awareness of theories and issues relating to the English language curriculum and to help them develop informed and critical approaches to recent and current developments in this area so that they can conduct research, plan and implement effective improvements in their teaching and offer informed leadership in the school. The course offers a critical review of current and recent developments in EFL curricula world wide and in Hong Kong with reference to changing perceptions of the nature of knowledge and the nature of language. The stated aims of education, societal and economic needs, language assessment issues and the design and implementation of language curriculum innovations. The course provides the foundation in the theories and research related to modern EFL curricula necessary for informed, critical and pro-active consumers of research in this rapidly-changing field.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA English Language Teaching
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the end of the course, learners will be able:
• To understand the relevant issues to the English language curriculum
• To develop informed and critical approaches to recent and current developments in the area
• To identify and critically analyse features of the local English language curriculum
• To compare and contrast the local curriculum with that of other countries
• To critically examine curriculum components
• To plan and design effective ELT curriculum programme in their own teaching context

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)
courses.course_syllabus:
1. The definitions of ‘a curriculum’ and related concepts such as syllabi
2. Issues related to the English language curriculum including its recent and current development
3. Analyses of features of the English language curricula in Hong Kong
4. Analyses of the English language curricula in Hong Kong and those from one or more countries
5. Designing ELT curricula

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Instruction and Assessment in Science Education
courses.description: This course reviews the research and findings on the relationship between effective teaching and student learning, leading to an understanding of the development of instructional strategies in different science programmes. This is followed by a consideration of the nature and impact of children’s misconceptions and pedagogical implications. The course also analyses the roles of practical work in school science and the current trends of teaching practical work. Different approaches of assessing achievement in science learning are considered, including that of practical work. It also explores the rationale, problems and future of assessment in science education, including the impact of the teacher assessment scheme.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Positive Education: Enhancing Students’ Learning and Well-being
courses.description: This course will explore the application of positive psychology and education principles in the school context. Participants will learn about how positive psychology/education can enhance well-being, motivation, engagement, and achievement among students and educators. Throughout the course, participants will engage in discussions, case studies, and class activities to deepen their understanding and practical application of positive psychology/education in educational settings. By the end of the course, participants will be equipped with a comprehensive toolkit of evidence-based strategies and interventions to create positive and thriving educational environments. They will have the knowledge and skills to enhance student well-being, motivation, engagement, and achievement, while also promoting their own professional growth and satisfaction as educators.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Eng Lang Teach, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu, PhD Edu & Master of Education Teach Key Sub Area (NETTI)
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students are expected to

• Recognize the different theories and models that underpin the field of positive psychology and education
• Understand the basic principles of positive psychology applied to educational settings
• Apply the principles of positive education to foster positive learning environments
• Evaluate the different positive psychology/education theories and models in terms of their applicability to their personal and professional lives
• Design their own positive psychology/education initiatives to promote students’/educators’ learning and/or well-being

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Positive Psychology Applied to Educational Settings
• What is positive psychology and positive education?
• How can positive education be applied to students and educators?
Background context of student and teacher well-being

Week 2, 3, 4: Fostering Happiness and Well-being among Students and Educators
• Broaden-and-build theory of positive emotions
• Different conceptualizations of well-being
• Student well-being
• Teacher well-being

Week 5, 6, 7: Nurturing Students’ Character Strengths
• Strengths of wisdom
• Strengths of courage
• Strengths of humanity
• Strengths of justice
• Strengths of temperance
• Strengths of transcendence

Week 8, 9, 10: Social and Emotional Learning
• Collaborative for Social and Emotional Learning
• Big Five Theory of Socio-emotional Skills
• Other models of social and emotional learning

Week 11, 12: Resilience and Growth Mindset
• Internal and external factors related to resilience
• Implicit theories of intelligence
• Stress mindsets
• Emotion mindsets

Week 13: Creating Positive Learning Environments
• Gratitude interventions
• Resilience interventions
• School belonging interventions
• Growth mindset interventions

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Effective School Leadership
courses.description: This course aims to encourage students to reflect upon what constitutes effective leadership in schools, especially within a constantly shifting reform environment, and to construct their own ‘theories’ of effective leadership. A range of established and emerging approaches and understandings of leadership are explored and analyzed in relation to the assorted contexts within which leaders lead. While recognizing the importance of the principalship the course assumes that leadership resides and operates across various levels within and outside traditional school boundaries and that teacher leadership is an important area of study. Areas covered include strategic and ethical leadership, panel leadership, leadership for learning and comparative, cross-cultural understandings of leadership. The course attempts to locate school leadership at the local level within a broader national, regional and international perspective. Through a combination of case studies, journal writing, problem-based cases, experiential activities and self-guided learning students apply leadership knowledge to their own personal and school contexts.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, you will be able to:
1. gain a firm understanding of the latest school leadership literature.
2. discuss a number of key personal and systemic leadership issues with fellow educators.
3. formally and informally reflect upon leadership and develop a supported stance of what effective leadership means in YOUR context.
4. apply the theories learnt to tackle leadership problems or issues.
5. critique the leadership practice that you experience or encounter.

meetings.instructor:
Dr. CHU Kai Wing, 
Dr. HO Sai Mun Stanley

Dr. CHU Kai Wing, 
Dr. HO Sai Mun Stanley

courses.description: This course is designed to familiarize students with theoretical and practical aspects of organizational change and educational planning. The course explores the current context of organizational change and introduces a number of theories and perspectives on change relevant to educational leaders in Hong Kong. Various approaches to educational planning for change are presented and applied. Particular attention is paid to school development planning for self-managing schools and the development of staff in these organizations. The course will include: (1) current theories and perspectives on organizational change; (2) current approaches to and techniques for implementing school development planning; (3) critique and application of change and planning models to relevant situations. Learning activities employed include case studies, solving problems, scenario writing and experience sharing. Through these activities students will develop conceptual and practical skills for the effective management of change in schools and other educational institutions.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Building Learning Communities
courses.description: This course introduces key concepts and issues in teacher and school development and the construction of professional learning communities and schools as learning organizations. Students are encouraged to think critically about the ways in which people are supported, rewarded and developed in schools and to reflect upon their own beliefs and experiences about leading people and working with others in different organizational contexts. The course assumes that leadership operates at multiple levels throughout the school. The course aims to bring together theoretical knowledge and critical application through utilizing group work, traditional lectures, web-based resources, self-guided learning and journal writing. General topic areas, depending on contextual demands, will include understandings of self-management and self as a learner, leading retention and rewards, optimizing professional and organizational capacity, focusing personal and professional development on student needs, communication and feedback, knowledge management, performance management and team growth, development and learning.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.

courses.description: The course aims to help students to develop a basic scholarly foundation for policy studies in education. It is designed to introduce students to basic concepts, perspectives and approaches in educational policy studies. The course will explicate the different conceptions of educational policy as well as its content, process and effects. It will also introduce students to various theoretical perspectives that explore the nature of various dimensions of educational policy. There may include humanist, liberal and communitarian perspectives. Students will be exposed to different policy approaches, which define the orientations of inquiry underlying various policy designs. These approaches will include various normative and disciplinary approaches to policy studies. Different policy conceptions, perspectives and approaches will be illustrated with examples of research and practice in both local and international contexts.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Education and Development in International Perspectives
courses.description: The course is designed to help students to study education and development from an international perspective. It will explicate how individual nation-states formulate their education policies within their particular political and historical context and how these policies are implemented in order to address social, economic, cultural and political issues in national development. Students will explore the common agendas governing the formulation and implementation of education policies from a comparative perspective. The course aims to expound the divergent and convergent trends among national education policies. The following issues will be examined: quality and equality of educational system, educational decentralization, relationship among home, school and community, globalization and education reform.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course will cover (but are not limited to) the following topics:
1. Education & Development: Definitions & Dimensions
2. Monitoring Educational Quality from an International Perspective
3. Analyzing Quality and Equality of Educational System of Hong Kong From an International Perspective
4. Study of Educational Effectiveness from an International Perspective
5. Educational Development Issues from Sociological Perspective (e.g. Educational Decentralization and School Based Management; Educational Reform and Parentocracy; School Improvement and Self-regulated learning; Expansion of Higher Education
6. Educational Development Issues from Political Perspective: Nation-building and Education of Citizens; Political Recruitment and Political Elites; Credential inflation; Shadow education;
7. Educational Development Issues from Economic Perspective: Economic Measurement on Development and Education; The Global Landscape of Education; Education and Economic Growth; Migration, Education of Migrants and Their Children; Education and Demographic Change

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Quantitative Methods in Educational Research
courses.description: This course is designed to introduce students to research methods, which approach educational phenomena from empirical and positivistic perspectives. The course will begin with the basic logic of conceptualization, operationalization and measurement in quantifying educational phenomena. It will explicate basic principles of research designs in quantitative methods. These may include experimental and quasi-experimental designs and sampling designs in survey methods. It will also familiarize students with basic operations of descriptive and inferential statistical analyses in educational research. The course content will be illustrated with cases of quantitative research in Hong Kong educational settings.

courses.learning_outcomes:
• Students will demonstrate knowledge of the basic concepts, theoretical perspectives, and debates about quantitative research methodology.

• Students will be able to recognize main threats to external and internal validity, and devise solutions to overcome these problems in research design.
• Students will be able to critically evaluate published research reports and design their own small research projects.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Qualitative Methods in Educational Research
courses.description: Education is a human activity of understanding and emancipation. Education begins with mutual understanding among teachers, students and knowledge. Its primary aim is to emancipate learners’ potentials. Understand the natures and features of processes of understanding and emancipation is, therefore, vital to education in general and educational research in particular. This course is design to familiarize students with the theoretical and practical aspects of research methods, which are design to reveal the understanding and emancipatory features of education. Topics to be covered may include: (1) the nature of qualitative research in education; (2) the theoretical foundation of qualitative research in education: hermeneutics, phenomenology and critical theory; (3) methodological features of major branches of qualitative research: ethnography, historical-comparative study, and reflective and critical action research; and (4) case studies of major branches of qualitative research in Hong Kong educational settings.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, MEd, MPhil Edu, MA Eng Lang Teach, MSc Math Edu, MA Sch Guid & Counsel, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, MA Prof Edu Psy, Doctor of Edu, PhD Edu & MEd Teach Key Sub Area (NETTI)
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, participants will
1) familiarize with different qualitative research methods
2) understand the process of collecting qualitative data
3) be able to grasp the basic skills to understand, appreciate and conduct qualitative research
4) be able to think reflexively while engaged in the research process

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Quantitative Data Management and Analysis in Educational Research
courses.description: This course is designed to introduce students to basic analytical skills commonly used in quantitative methods in educational research. It will begin by explicating the nature and features of different types of data in quantitative analysis. They include binary, nominal, ordinal and continuous data. The course then will discuss different methods of univariate and bivariate analyses. Various methods in inferential statistics will also be expounded. In the course, computer programmes commonly used in quantitative analyses of educational and social data, such as SPSS, will be introduced.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Eng Lang Teach, MSc Math Edu, MA Sch Guid & Counsel, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, MA Prof Edu Psy, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students will be able to:
• gain knowledge and understanding of major concepts, theoretical perspectives, empirical applications of basic statistical and data analyses used in educational research.
• identify appropriate statistical tests and techniques for their own research questions and hypotheses.
• carry out basic statistical tests.

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)

Measurements, distributions & descriptive statistics (means, variance, standard deviation)

Statistical tests of differences between means: T-test, ANOVA

Correlation
Introduction to SPSS

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Multi-dimensional and Multi-causal Analysis in Educational Research
courses.description:
This course introduces students to multivariate techniques and survey methods to examine complex relations and associations that often interact with or overlap one another in education. Structural equation modeling, factor analysis, and large-scale assessment and survey techniques will be introduced to analyse multi-dimensionality and multi-causality of educational phenomena.
The present course will build on concepts and techniques learnt in PEDU6401 (Quantitative Methods in Educational Research) and PEDU6403 (Quantitative Data Management and Analysis in Educational Research).
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Eng Lang Teach, MSc Math Edu, MA Sch Guid & Counsel, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, MA Prof Edu Psy, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu;Pre-requisite: PEDU6403
courses.course_syllabus:
Basic concepts related to confirmatory factor analysis and structural equation modeling will be discussed. Specifically, the course will cover the following topics:
1. difference between exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis, meaning of model fitting;
2. specification and analyses of common models, such as confirmatory factor model, multitrait-multimethod model, full structural equation model, high-order factor model, simplex model, multiple group model; and
3. issues related to data types (e.g., nonnormality) and model fitting (e.g., single indicator).

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Action Research in Education
courses.description: Action research has been identified as one of the major means to help teachers to reflect on their professional practices and to critically assess the sources and consequences of their practices. This course aims to provide participants with the knowledge and skills to conduct rigorous action research to improve their own teaching practices.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Project and Research Report: Educational Administration and Policy
courses.description: Students in their second year, with the guidance of their supervisors, have to conduct a small project investigating one significant issue or question related to one of the following areas: 1) school-based management; 2) education policy and decision analysis; 3) leadership and organizational culture; 4) roles and tasks of educational administrator; 5) school effectiveness and school improvement; 6) environmental constraints and impacts on educational administration; 7) accountability and ethical aspects of educational administration; 8) personnel management; 9) educational planning and school finance; 10) decision-making in education; and 11) others (with permission of supervisor). In general, this project may be a survey, an experimental study, or a case study. Students have to complete a research report of the project according to a standard format given by the Faculty. With the permission of their supervisors, it is possible for a group of not more than three students to conduct one substantial project.

courses.learning_outcomes:

3. Integrate the skills and knowledge which have been taught in the degree programme with specific application to a topic to produce a well-argued report.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Project in Learning Sciences and Technologies
courses.description:
This is a two-semester course in which students will work in groups (2 to 3 students per group) to pursue a learning design project for further developing and applying their knowledge and skills in learning sciences and technologies. With the guidance of a project supervisor, each group will identify a specific learning problem/ issue and apply appropriate learning theories to create a technology-enhanced learning environment (or other “products” such as a technology-enhanced curriculum or pedagogy) for addressing the problem/ issue identified. At the end of the first and second semesters respectively, every group should submit an interim report and a final report of the design project. In addition to the self-arranged meetings with their supervisors, all groups are required to attend four compulsory seminar-based lectures for the following purposes:
1. Introducing the learning design projects (at the beginning of the 1st semester)
2. Presenting the project proposals (at the beginning of the 2nd month of the 1st semester)
3. Presenting the interim project reports (at the beginning of the 2nd semester)
4. Presenting the final project reports (at the end of the 2nd semester)

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Gifted Education and Talent Development: Concepts and Approaches
courses.description: This course introduces students to current concepts and approaches in the field of gifted education and talent development. Issues in the evolution of the conceptions of giftedness and talents, creativity, and eminence will be discussed with reference to curriculum and programming, teaching, parenting, and counseling gifted and talented children and youth. Special considerations will be given to the development of gifted education and talent development in Hong Kong and Chinese societies.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students are expected to be able to:
• understand the history, development, and key concepts of gifted education and talent development in Western countries and Chinese societies particularly in Hong Kong;
• identify and examine the specific issues relevant to giftedness, intelligence, creativity, leadership, and eminence;
• understand and address the unique characteristics and needs of gifted and talented students
• understand how theories can be put into practice through examining parenting, teaching, programming and counseling gifted and talented students; and
• appreciate the need to integrate different pathways in a meaningful way for application to nurturing the gifted and talented in educational practice.

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)
courses.course_syllabus:
– Invitation to gifted education and talent development: Introduction and conceptual overview
– Course outline and assessment scheme
– Giftedness and intelligence
– Characteristics of gifted children/ students
– Characteristics of creative persons
– Leadership and case sharing
– Leadership development
– Musical and artistic talents
– Learning needs of gifted students: Differentiation, models and approaches in gifted education
– Learning needs of gifted students: Teaching, programming & mentoring
– Special populations: Prodigies, savants & the twice exceptional gifted
– Assessing & identifying gifted students
– Social and emotional needs of gifted children/ students: Parenting & counseling
– Parenting gifted children and home-school-community collaboration for gifted students
– Counseling and career guidance needs of the gifted and talented
– Summary and important messages

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Motivating the Academically Unmotivated
courses.description: In this course, we examine current theories of students' motivational behaviour in the classroom. We particularly focus on cognitive theories of achievement motivation, attributional processes, goal orientations, self-concept, intrinsic/ extrinsic motivation, self- regulated learning, and cross-cultural studies on students' motivational behaviour. After critically considering the research evidence for these theories, we discuss how to apply them to classroom teaching.

courses.description: In this course, theoretical bases and recent developments in specific research areas will be introduced by scholars of educational psychology. courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Learning Sciences and Instructional Implications
courses.description: This course provides a general overview on theories of learning sciences and their applications in education. First, the basics of human cognition are introduced, including but not limited to attention, short/working/long-term memory, knowledge representation, meaningful learning, cognitive processes of encoding, retrieval, and forgetting. Second, evidence-based instructional strategies/principles based on research in learning sciences are discussed, such as mental imagery, attention/memory training, feedback, worked examples, spacing, interleaving, influence of mood and anxiety, metacognition/self-regulation, and practice testing. Third, the implications of the psychological theories on the design and evaluation of technology-enhanced learning environments are elaborated.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MSc Math Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu, PhD Edu & Master of Education Teach Key Sub Area (NETTI)
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students should be able to:
(a) demonstrate an understanding of psychological theories of learning sciences,
(b) describe higher-order cognition and use metacognitive strategies to improve educational practices,
(c) examine research design and key findings in psychological studies of learning sciences and highlight their implications for the design and evaluation of technology-enhanced learning environments, and
(d) apply effective evidence-based instructional strategies based on research in learning sciences to foster students’ learning.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Higher Order Thinking in Education
courses.description: Students of the 21st century are expected to be creative and have an inquiring mind; and be able to think critically and solve problems both independently and collaboratively. These high order thinking skills enable students to construct new knowledge from the overabundant information and to make critical decisions in the digital era. This course aims to provide participants with a fundamental understanding of higher order thinking skills (such as critical thinking, problem-solving, and creativity) from multiple theoretical perspectives (e.g., philosophical, cognitive, social-psychological, neuroscience and pedagogical) and an in-depth exploration of how to foster the use of higher order thinking skills in schools and other educational settings.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu, PhD Edu & Master of Education Teach Key Sub Area (NETTI)
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Definitions and examples of higher order thinking skills:
a. Critical thinking
b. Logical reasoning
c. Reflective thinking
d. Metacognition
e. Creative thinking
f. Problem solving
g. Decision making
2. Latest research (philosophical, cognitive, social-psychological, neuroscience and pedagogical) findings on these higher order thinking skills
3. How to foster the use of these higher order thinking skills in schools and other educational settings

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Educating Students with Special Needs
courses.description: This course aims to provide students with theoretical foundations of selected areas of special needs and help them to understand possible learning difficulties children with these special needs would encounter. These areas of special needs include: 1) learning disabilities; 2) giftedness and talents; 3) autism spectrum disorder; 4) attention deficit hyperactivity disorder; and 5) emotional and behavioral disorders. For each kind of special need, students will learn its characteristics, causes, and provision of special education in Hong Kong. Besides, this course will also prepare students for developing essential intervention strategies and techniques to help children with learning difficulties. Students will also work in small groups, under the supervision of the instructor, to conduct case studies in order to strengthen their knowledge of the learning difficulties of selected groups of children and adolescents.

At the end of the semester, students should be able to
1. acquire basic principles of identification and education of various types of special-needs children
2. have an understanding of provision of special education in Hong Kong
3. critically evaluate current issues related to the education of students with special educational needs
4. develop relevant intervention strategies to facilitate student development and enhance learning in primary school years

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Family-School-Community Collaboration: From Theory and Research to Practice
courses.description: The purpose of the course is to provide a comprehensive account of the nature and impact of family-school-community (FSC) collaboration in education. The course will examine the relationship between family, school, and community for children‘s education, parents empowerment and school improvement. For the past two decades, growing evidence shows that parents are valuable but largely untapped resources for the field of education. In Hong Kong, FSC collaboration is of particular interest to policy makers and educators because it may have considerable potential to address three major problems in public education: scarcity of resources; rigidity of bureaucracy; and quality and inequality of educational outcomes. The course will help participants understand how to obtain higher and better participation from all families regardless of their background; and to enhance the inclusive attitude of educators (including teachers, social workers, etc.) to address the diverse needs of parents and their children.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Overview of Family-community-school collaboration
2. Family Deficiency, Institutional Discrimination and Inclusive Model of FSC collaboration
3. Understanding the Logic of Practices of Parental Involvement from Different Background
4. Research on FSC: Qualitative, Quantitative and Mixed method to study the nature and Impact of FSC
5. Parental involvement in children's development with different needs (working with parents of gifted students, SEN students, enhancing students social emotional well being)
6. Home based involvement: Parenting and Learning at home
7. Innovative Ideas on Home school Communication
8. Parental Involvement in School Governance and Educational Decentralization
9. Community Involvement and BUS model
10. Strategic Plan for Family-community-school collaboration

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: The Psychology of Parenting
courses.description: Although there is a myriad of opinions on how parents are supposed to raise their children, they are often not based on scientific investigations of parenting. This course will systematically examine the theories and research on parenting cognitions and practices from a psychological perspective. We will examine different determinants of parenting, including parents' characteristics, children's characteristics, as well as the family and sociocultural contexts in which both parents and children are embedded. The effects of parenting on children will also be examined, with attention to the processes through which these effects occur.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.learning_outcomes:
A. Describe the major theories and empirical findings in the field of parenting.
B. Understand the antecedents of different parenting cognitions and practices and their effects on children’s psychological functioning.
C. Describe and discuss the methods used by researchers to study parenting.
D. Conduct simple, systematic investigations of issues related to parenting.
E. Critique contemporary issues in parenting.
F. Identify ways to support parents to effectively fulfill their important role in children’s development

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Counselling for Professionals in Parent Education
courses.description: This course aims to introduce to teachers, school social workers and other professionals involved in parent education the basic principles and objectives of counselling as a helping profession. Within the framework of selected theoretical perspectives, students will gain insights into how family and its dynamics influence the development of self, values, communication patterns and so on. Counselling skills relevant to working with parents will be introduced and practiced. Professional and ethical codes, specifically those related to working with parents will be addressed. Contemporary issues/ trends related to the fields of counselling and parent education will also be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Chi Lang Edu, MA Early Child Edu, Master of Edu, MPhil Edu, MA Sch Improve & Leader, MPhil Sports Sci & Phys Edu, Doctor of Edu & PhD Edu.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon satisfactory completion of the course, students are expected to be able to:
1. describe the basic principles and objectives of counselling as a helping profession,
2. analyze how family dynamics lead to the development of the self, values and communication patterns etc.,
3. apply the basic counselling concepts/ skills introduced to their work with parents,
4. critique issues/ trends related to the fields of counselling and parent education.

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)
courses.course_syllabus:
Basic principles and objectives of counselling as a helping profession, ideas and concepts in understanding family dynamics and parents’ concerns, selected theories in counselling psychology, basic counselling skills, issues/ trends related to the fields of counselling and parent education etc.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Curriculum Organization
courses.description: Different approaches in organizing school curricula are based on different rationale and conceptualization concerning curriculum activities in schools. The nature and forms of knowledge constitute an important component in understanding and designing school curricula. Other relevant factors include school structure and culture, teachers’ subject identity and status of subject, etc. In this course, the different ways of curriculum organization are delineated and evaluated in the light of epistemological considerations. Analysing the different areas of human experience and knowledge should lead to a reconceptualization of the task of organizing curriculum from a higher standpoint. In this course, the design and implementation problems of different ways of curriculum organization, such as discipline-based curriculum, integrated curriculum and modular curriculum in different stages of education will be studied. This exercise of reflection should better prepare course participants for the practical task of curriculum design.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in PhD Education; Doctor of Education
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1

Topic: Introduction to curriculum organization and curriculum studies & students’ doctoral projects sharing

Content: Key concepts in curriculum studies

Week 2

Topic: Ideologies and philosophies that shape models of curriculum organization 1: At policy level

Cases: Hong Kong curriculum structure/Mainland suyang-curriculum reform

Content: Features of current curriculum policies global wise

# 1: lead discussion on Pinar (2008)

Week 3

Topic: Ideologies and philosophies that shape models of curriculum organization 2: At curriculum design and instruction level

Cases: Tyler Rationale, Backward Design, and University Alignment Model

Content: Features of current curriculum practices global wise

# 2: lead discussion on Kliebard and Huebner’s article

Week 4

Topic: Ideological and power struggles in curriculum organization I: Core curriculum & hidden curriculum

Case: Michael Apple’s official knowledge

Content: Why certain knowledge gets taught in schools as official knowledge?

# 3: lead discussion on Apple’s book

Week 5

Topic: Ideological and power struggles in curriculum organization II: Teacher-centered vs. Student-centered curriculum organization

Case: Michel Foucault’s panopticon & discipline and punish

Content: Governmentality and teacher-student ordering

# 4: lead discussion on Foucault’s book

Week 6

Topic: Ideological and power struggles in curriculum organization III: Knowledge vs. skills curriculum

Case: Liberal Studies at a crossroads

Content: Pedagogical dilemma and curriculum organization

# 5: lead discussion on HK liberal studies curriculum reform policies

Week 7

Topic: Ideological and power struggles in curriculum organization IV: Integrated vs. subject curriculum

Case: STE(A)M, Reading across curriculum

Content: Integration vs. mixture challenges

# 6: lead discussion on HK STEM and reading across curriculum

Week 8

Topic: Other issues in curriculum organization 1: Culture and nation boundaries

Case: Bohunt school incident & curriculum epistemicide

Content: East-West dialogue

# 7: lead discussion on Paraskeva’s book

Week 9

Topic: Other issues in curriculum organization 2: Diversity, inclusion, equity, and differentiated mode of instruction

Case: HK’s inclusive education

Content: Difference and identity

# 8: lead discussion on Deleuze’s article in relation to HK inclusive curriculum challenges

Week 10

Topic: Other issues in curriculum organization 3: Student voices, wellbeing & social conscience

Guest speaker: Marty Schmidt (TBD)

Content: Wellbeing and social consciousness

Week 11

Topic: Course review and final paper presentations 1

Week 12

Topic: Course review and final paper presentations 2

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Curriculum Inquiry: Paradigms
courses.description: This course is designed for those students who wish to have in-depth explorations of curriculum issues from different paradigms, namely Traditionalism/ Conceptual- empiricism/ Reconceptualism, and Perennial Analytic/ Practical Inquiry/ Critical Praxis paradigm. The course focuses on: 1) the application of above paradigms on the decisions of the nature and relationship of curriculum common places, such as the selection of aims and objectives, learning opportunities, teachers, learners and teaching/ learning environment, and social milieu/ context; and 2) the application of above paradigms in understanding the processes of curriculum development, implementation and evaluation as well as in designing modes of curriculum inquiry.

1. describe the advanced theories and issues of curriculum inquiry;

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Foundations of Educational Administration and Policy
courses.description: This is a foundational course on contemporary education theories that prepares doctoral students to engage with theories in educational research. The course extends beyond the scope of “educational administration and policy” but considers, broadly, the epistemological, theoretical and methodological considerations of applying theory in educational research. The course is structured around four themes with each block focusing on a few key theorists and their theoretical ideas. The overall aim of the course is to induct and provoke doctoral students to think theoretically and reflexively with theory for their doctoral dissertation and future educational research.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in PhD Education; Doctor of Education
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the end of this course, students should be able to:
1. Understand key foundational theories introduced in the course.
2. Understand how use theory in educational research.
3. Apply Western theory critically in educational context in Asia.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: School Reform in a Global Context
courses.description: This course aims to provide an understanding of the major contextual forces that have shaped local school reform. It seeks to investigate the dynamics of school reform as a global phenomenon and analyze its impact on educational policy and administration at the international, societal, and institutional levels. School reform policies are often viewed as endeavors to enhance national competitiveness in the world arena. New administrative measures can also be perceived as means to attain efficiency and effectiveness. These will be examined. By referring to the experiences of countries, mostly developed ones, which have influenced the direction of local school reform, and drawing insights from relevant research findings, this course will address critical issues in key areas of school reform in this part of the world. Topics to be discussed in this course will include: change in conception of human capital and reform in educational administration, school governance, resource allocation, curricula and teaching, quality assurance, teacher and principal preparations, leadership practices and so on.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Advanced Seminar in Working and Report Writing with Large-Scale Educational Surveys
courses.description: In this course, we will (1) introduce advanced methods to analyze quantitative data, including the use of data from large-scale educational surveys, (2) discuss how to find important and interesting stories from the data, and (3) learn to write research reports that are publishable in academic journals.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Doctor of Philosophy in Education;For students in Doctor of Education with Prerequisite: PEDU6401
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Students will demonstrate knowledge of the basic concepts, debates, and skills in various aspects of quantitative analyses.
* Students will be able to identify and unpack significant educational phenomena from a given set of variables, including stating the significance, non-trivialness of possible research questions, and possible analytical approaches.
* Students will be able to critically evaluate published research reports and improve on the analytical procedures to come up with additional research questions.
* Students will be able to write a manuscript that is ready for submission with the potential of getting published.

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)
courses.course_syllabus:
Despite the limitations of survey research, it provides well-accepted norms and references for the continuous improvement of education. This course equips doctoral students with advanced statistical techniques (e.g., multiple regression, ANCOVA, MANOVA, repeated measures MANOVA, principal components and factor analysis, confirmatory factor analysis, structural equation modeling, multilevel/ hierarchical linear models, brief coverage: logistic regression, discriminant analysis, canonical analysis). As large-scale national or international surveys (e.g., PISA, TIMSS, and NAEP) have become increasingly important, these high quality data will be used for hands-on practice together with any other high quality quantitative data that students might have collected themselves.

Potential research topics include: (1) comparing Hong Kong (or other Asian economies) against western economies in the recent cycle, (2) examining the trends of Hong Kong in the last two decades, (3) examining the universality of psychological or educational theories across many economies, and (4) examining the relations among various aspects of teaching, family background, parental practices, and students’ well-being. Students are responsible for linking the data analyses with appropriate theoretical frameworks.

The course will provide a basic introduction to advanced analytical procedures. Then students will have to (1) identify suitable constructs in the appropriate surveys, (2) formulate hypotheses, (3) conduct analyses and explore relations, and (4) draw up conclusions and write up results for publication. Students have to make frequent presentations and actively discuss classmates’ proposals and results.

Students are expected to have acquired fundamental research knowledge before enrolling PEDU7303.
Pre-requisites of PEDU7303 for DEd students: PEDU6401

courses.title: Advanced Seminar in Data Analysis and Writing for Qualitative Research

meetings.instructor:
Professor TSANG Wing Kwong, 
Professor WONG Lai Ngok Jocelyn

Professor TSANG Wing Kwong, 
Professor WONG Lai Ngok Jocelyn

courses.description:
This course is designed for doctoral students who want to conduct a qualitative research project. The course will offer the multifaceted and advanced knowledge needed for such work, plus core techniques commonly used in the qualitative research paradigm. Providing students with a comprehensive and sophisticated understanding of philosophical stances underpinning different qualitative methodologies will help them situate their research and shape the deployment of their qualitative inquiry in an informed manner.
The course will concentrate on developing students' knowledge and practical skills for conducting qualitative research in the following areas: (1) the use of relevant explanatory and exploratory research questions in different qualitative studies; (2) writing a critical literature review and theoretical sensitivity; (3) formulating a research protocol and the practice of triangulation; (4) hands-on experiences of collecting qualitative data with relevant data collection techniques; (5) data interpretation and analysis through coding practices based on the content analysis approach; (6) having theory building with the application of grounded theory; (7) developing skills to draw conclusions and write reports, both of which serve as the basis for preparing a doctoral research proposal and/or thesis; (8) special attention will be given to the issue of ‘researcher as instrument’ and related ethical issues. To put the knowledge acquired into practice, students will be required to undertake a mini qualitative project individually with rigorous research design. It is anticipated that students will gain the competency to interpret and report their research findings in an academic manner.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Doctor of Education and Doctor of Philosophy in Education;Prerequisite: PEDU6402
courses.learning_outcomes:
After the completion of the course, students will be able to:

1. Have advanced knowledge of qualitative inquiry by understanding the philosophical and theoretical foundations underpinning different qualitative research methodologies;
2. Plan good quality qualitative research by having a thoughtful research design and research protocol;
3. Write a critical literature review, addressing the research gap through reviewing updated empirical studies and research reports in both international and local contexts
4. Develop and apply data collection skills with an awareness of practical and ethical factors related to conducting research activities
5. Understand the complexity of qualitative analysis and its interpretation in reference to relevant literature
6. Generate, write and report qualitative research findings at a level suitable for a research proposal and/or thesis.

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Selected Topics in the Science of Learning
courses.description: Is scientific approach in educational research possible? What constitute scientific evidence in educational research? In recent years, even though the call for more evidence-based educational practices has become ubiquitous, new instructional innovations and practices have been implemented faster than the accumulation of research evidence. In this course, we will first discuss the meaning of scientific approach in educational research that have shaped the current landscape of the field. Then, we will discuss how this approach enhances the understanding of recent instructional innovations and practices for the improvement of students’ learning. Students of this course are expected to learn about both “form” (i.e., methodology) and “substance” (i.e., content) of educational research by critical analysis of the literature in order to become prepared for leading roles in instructional innovations and practices.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in PhD Education; Doctor of Education
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students are expected to

1. Demonstrate the ability to critically analyze and evaluate recent advances in educational research in the fields of learning and teaching

2. Demonstrate the ability to apply ideas derived from educational research into practice

Both learning outcomes are assessed via the discussion, seminar presentations, and individual term paper.

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Scientific research in education

2. Review of scientific research

3. Analyses of research on instructional practice and innovations (e.g., learning styles, flipped classroom, and E-learning)

4. The myth and reality of brain-based education

5. How science advances special education

6. Examining learning and teaching from cognitive, social and affective perspectives

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: Doctoral Independent Study/Independent Study
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to provide an opportunity for Ed.D. students, under the guidance of their supervisors, to conduct independent study within their specializations and/ or other specializations according to their research interests. The course enables the students to pursue needed study in a field in which appropriate courses are not being offered within the normative period of study. At the completion of the course, students are expected to have enriched their knowledge in their specializations.

courses.learning_outcomes:
Students can only take this course when a required course is not offered during the normative study period. Students will be under the guidance of supervisors to conduct an independent study within their specializations and/ or other specializations according to their research interests to enrich the knowledge in their chosen specializations.

courses.subject: PEDU
courses.title: M.Phil. Seminar in Educational Studies I
courses.description: Students have to fulfill all the course requirements and satisfy the required attendance. This course is designed to familiarize students with the principles, structure and process of education research, and it is organized into two parts. The first part is in the form of lecture which takes up the first seven sessions of the courses. It aims to provide students with a general understanding of the nature and substance of education research. Thus, topics to be covered in these lectures are major paradigms in education inquiry, the philosophical and ethical issues relating to education research, and the basic principles and procedures in education research. The second part of the course is organized in the form of seminar during the two semesters. Each seminar is conducted by a research student in the Division of Education and is supervised by no less than two members of the teaching staff of the Faculty of Education. Depending on the research interests of the research students conducting the seminars, research ideas and plans in educational studies will be critically reviewed and discussed. (For M.Phil. students only.)
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil Education; Mphil Sports Sci. & Phy. Edu.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are provided with an understanding of the nature and substance of education research. Students are also required to conduct research seminars according to their research interests.

(Individual course teachers may update the learning outcome as and whenever necessary.)
